title: Dementia with Lewy Bodies
authors: Irene Litvan, William W. Seeley, Bruce L. Miller

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Diagnostic Features and Timing Differentiate PDD and DLB
items.content: The timing of cognitive decline relative to parkinsonism is crucial in differentiating Parkinson's disease dementia (PDD) from dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB). In PDD, patients have a history of long-standing Parkinson's disease before developing dementia. Conversely, in DLB, dementia and neuropsychiatric symptoms either precede or occur simultaneously with the onset of parkinsonism.
keyPoints: PDD: Dementia occurs after established Parkinson's disease.
DLB: Dementia co-emerges with or precedes parkinsonism.
Neuropsychiatric symptoms in DLB include visual hallucinations and fluctuating attention.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Characteristic Cognitive and Neuropsychiatric Features of LBD
items.content: Lewy body dementia (LBD) manifests with severe executive, attentional, and visuospatial deficits while preserving episodic memory. Fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder (RBD), and parkinsonism are key diagnostic features in DLB.
keyPoints: Severe executive, attentional, and visuospatial deficits with preserved episodic memory.
Fluctuating cognition can lead to marked short-term confusion or lethargy.
Visual hallucinations and RBD are common neuropsychiatric symptoms.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Differentiating DLB from Other Neurodegenerative Disorders
items.content: DLB can be challenging to differentiate from other neurodegenerative disorders like multiple system atrophy (MSA) and Alzheimer's disease (AD). Orthostatic hypotension, early autonomic disturbances, and anosmia are more characteristic of LBD than MSA. Episodic memory disturbance suggests comorbid AD.
keyPoints: Orthostatic hypotension and early autonomic disturbances can occur in DLB.
Anosmia is more characteristic of LBD compared to MSA.
Episodic memory disturbance indicates possible comorbid Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Prodromal Phase Characteristics in LBD
items.content: In the prodromal phase of Lewy body diseases (LBD), patients exhibit mild cognitive impairment with specific deficits. Prodromal DLB includes executive, attention, and visuospatial disturbances alongside hallucinations, RBD, fluctuations in attention, or parkinsonism. Differentiating between MCI-DLB and PD-MCI can be challenging when major features are RBD and parkinsonism; thus, the term prodromal MCI-Lewy body (MCI-LB) is used. Biomarkers assist in distinguishing LBD from AD.
keyPoints: Prodromal phase involves mild cognitive impairment (MCI).
PD-MCI: deficits in executive function, attention, visuospatial disturbances; can be amnestic or multiple-domain MCI.
Prodromal DLB includes similar cognitive issues plus hallucinations, RBD, fluctuations in attention, parkinsonism.
RBD may precede LBD-related syndromes by years, evolving into PD or DLB.
Term 'prodromal MCI-Lewy body' proposed for differentiation when major features are RBD and parkinsonism.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · PRODROMAL PHASE | PATHOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Pathology of Lewy Body Dementia
items.content: Lewy bodies, the hallmark of LBD pathology, are found in specific brain regions and consist of $\alpha$-synuclein aggregates. These inclusions stain with PAS and ubiquitin and contain neurofilament proteins. Neuronal and synaptic loss predict clinical deficits more than Lewy pathology itself. PD progression follows a path from the enteric nervous system to cortical areas, potentially spreading via a prion-like mechanism involving $\alpha$-synuclein.
keyPoints: Lewy bodies found in brainstem nuclei, substantia nigra, amygdala, cingulate gyrus, neocortex.
Neuronal and synaptic loss predict clinical deficits more than Lewy pathology.
PD progression: enteric nervous system -> vagus nerve -> heart -> lower brainstem -> substantia nigra -> limbic system -> cerebral cortex.
$\alpha$-synuclein aggregates may propagate via a prion-like mechanism.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · PRODROMAL PHASE | PATHOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.question: Which cognitive deficits are most commonly associated with both Parkinson's disease dementia (PDD) and dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB)?
options: Severe executive, attentional, and visuospatial deficits but preserved episodic memory
Severe episodic memory loss with preserved executive function
Preserved cognitive function with motor symptoms only
Mild cognitive impairment without specific deficits
items.explanation: PDD and DLB usually manifest with severe executive, attentional, and visuospatial deficits but preserved episodic memory.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of rapid eye movement sleep behavior disorder (RBD) in patients with DLB?
options: Complete skeletal muscle paralysis during dreaming
items.explanation: In RBD, patients enact their dreams, often violently, leading to potential injuries.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.question: Which of the following symptoms is less frequent in DLB compared to hallucinations?
items.explanation: Delusions are less frequent than hallucinations and usually involve themes of misidentification or persecution.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.question: What is a distinguishing factor between PDD and DLB in terms of symptom onset?
options: DLB presents dementia after parkinsonism, while PDD presents them concurrently.
PDD presents dementia before parkinsonism, while DLB presents them concurrently.
Both present with dementia preceding parkinsonism.
In PDD, dementia follows long-standing PD; in DLB, dementia and neuropsychiatric symptoms precede or co-emerge with parkinsonism.
items.explanation: The time interval between the onset of parkinsonism and dementia differentiates PDD from DLB.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which cognitive deficits are characteristic of PD-MCI?
options: Deficits in executive, attention, and visuospatial disturbances
Memory loss without other cognitive impairments
items.explanation: PD-MCI is characterized by deficits in executive, attention, and visuospatial disturbances.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · PRODROMAL PHASE | PATHOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What are the key neuropathologic features of Lewy body disease (LBD)?
options: Presence of Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites throughout specific brain regions
Neuronal loss without any intraneuronal inclusions
items.explanation: The key neuropathologic feature in LBD is the presence of Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · PRODROMAL PHASE | PATHOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which protein aggregates are identified with antibodies to characterize Lewy bodies?
items.explanation: Lewy bodies are identified with antibodies to the presynaptic protein $\alpha$-synuclein.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · PRODROMAL PHASE | PATHOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Fluctuating attention and concentration are characteristic features of DLB.
items.explanation: DLB is characterized by marked fluctuations in cognitive functioning, including attention and concentration.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Episodic memory disturbance is a hallmark of DLB.
items.explanation: In LBD, episodic memory is usually preserved; its disturbance points to comorbid Alzheimer's disease.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: RBD may precede the development of an LBD-related syndrome by many years.
items.explanation: RBD can precede the development of either PD or DLB.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · PRODROMAL PHASE | PATHOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: PD usually begins in the cerebral cortex.
items.explanation: PD typically starts in the enteric nervous system and spreads to other areas, not beginning in the cerebral cortex.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · PRODROMAL PHASE | PATHOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.assertion: Patients with DLB are more sensitive to infectious or metabolic disturbances.
items.reason: The first manifestation of DLB in some patients is delirium, often precipitated by an infection or new medicine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why patients with DLB are sensitive to disturbances.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.assertion: Anosmia is more characteristic of multiple system atrophy (MSA) than Lewy body disease.
items.reason: Anosmia is a common feature in LBD, including DLB and PDD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because anosmia is more characteristic of LBD than MSA.
items.reference: Dementia with Lewy Bodies · Dementia with Lewy Bodies | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
